question:
6. The curve \( y=f(x) \) passes through the origin with a slope of 1 . It satisfies the differential equation \( \left(x^{2}+9\right) y^{\prime \prime}+\left(x^{2}+4\right) y=0 \). Show that it crosses the \( x \) axis between
\[
x=\frac{3}{2} \pi \quad \text { and } \quad x=\sqrt{\frac{63}{53}} \pi
\]
solution:
Solution. We shall use the Sturm comparison theorem. We state the theorem now and give the proof later.

Sturm Comparison Theorem. Let I be an interval in \( \mathbf{R} \) and suppose the functions \( u \) and \( v \) satisfy
\[
\begin{aligned}
u^{\prime \prime}(x)+A(x) u(x) & =0 \\
v^{\prime \prime}(x)+B(x) v(x) & =0
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( x \in I \), where \( A \) and \( B \) are continuous functions such that \( A(x) \geq B(x) \) for \( x \in I \). Assume \( v \) is not identically zero on I and let \( \alpha \) and \( \beta \) be zeros of \( v \) with \( \alpha<\beta \). Then there is a zero of \( u \) in the open interval \( (\alpha, \beta) \) unless \( A(x)=B(x) \) for \( \alpha \leq x \leq \beta \) and \( u \) and \( v \) are proportional in this interval.

For the stated problem we first compare the differential equations
\[
y^{\prime \prime}+\frac{x^{2}+4}{x^{2}+9} y=0
\]
and
\[
v^{\prime \prime}+\frac{4}{9} v=0 .
\]

As a solution of (2) we take \( \nu(x)=\sin \frac{2}{3} x \), which has zeros at 0 and \( \frac{5}{2} \pi \). Since
\[
\frac{x^{2}+4}{x^{2}+9} \geq \frac{4}{9}
\]
for all \( x \) with strict inequality for \( x \neq 0 \), any solution of (1) must have a zero in \( \left(0, \frac{3}{2} \pi\right) \). Hence there exists a \( \xi \in\left(0, \frac{3}{2} \pi\right) \) such that \( f(\xi)=0 \). Moreover, the graph of \( f \) must cross the \( x \)-axis at \( \xi \) because otherwise \( f^{\prime}(\xi)=0 \), and then the uniqueness theorem for solutions of (1) would imply that \( f(x)=0 \) for all \( x \).
Now for \( 0 \leq x \leq \frac{3}{2} \pi \), we have
\[
\frac{x^{2}+4}{x^{2}+9}<\frac{53}{63} .
\]

To see this, note that (3) is equivalent to \( 10 x^{2}<225 \). Since \( \pi^{2}<10 \), we see that \( 10\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}\right)^{2}<225 \), so (3) follows.
If we set
\[
u(x)=\sin \sqrt{\frac{53}{63}} x,
\]
then
\[
u^{\prime \prime}(x)+\frac{53}{63} u(x)=0 .
\]

Applying the Sturm comparison theorem again (with \( I=\left[0, \frac{3}{2} \pi\right] \) ), we conclude that \( u \) has a zero on \( (0, \xi) \). But the first positive zero of \( u \) is at \( \sqrt{63 / 53} \pi \), so \( \sqrt{63 / 53} \pi<\xi \). Thus we have
\[
\sqrt{\frac{63}{53}} \pi<\xi<\frac{3}{2} \pi
\]
as required.
Proof of the Sturm Comparison Theorem. The zeros of \( v \) are isolated, hence we may assume that \( \beta \) is the next zero after \( \alpha \); i.e., that \( v \) has a fixed sign on \( (\alpha, \beta) \). Suppose \( u \) has no zero in \( (\alpha, \beta) \). Then \( u \) also has a fixed sign on ( \( \alpha, \beta \) ). Changing the signs of \( u \) and/or \( v \) if necessary (which does not affect the location of the zeros), we may assume that both \( u \) and \( v \) are positive on \( (\alpha, \beta) \). It is then clear that \( v^{\prime}(\alpha) \geq 0 \) and \( v^{\prime}(\beta) \leq 0 \). A non-zero solution of a non-singular second-order linear differential equation and its derivative cannot both vanish at the same point, so we must have
\[
v^{\prime}(\alpha)>0, \quad v^{\prime}(\beta)<0 .
\]

Consider \( w(x)=u(x) v^{\prime}(x)-u^{\prime}(x) v(x) \). We have
\[
\begin{aligned}
w^{\prime}(x) & =u(x) v^{\prime \prime}(x)-u^{\prime \prime}(x) v(x) \\
& =(A(x)-B(x)) u(x) v(x) \geq 0
\end{aligned}
\]
for all \( x \in[\alpha, \beta] \). Therefore, \( w \) is non-decreasing on \( [\alpha, \beta] \), and in particular \( w(\alpha) \leq w(\beta) \). Then from the definition of \( w \) we obtain
\[
u(\alpha) v^{\prime}(\alpha) \leq u(\beta) v^{\prime}(\beta) .
\]

Comparing this with (4) and remembering that \( u(\alpha) \geq 0, u(\beta) \geq 0 \), we see that \( u(\alpha)=u(\beta)=0 \) and \( w(\alpha)=w(\beta)=0 \). But then, since \( w \) is monotone on \( [\alpha, \beta] \), both \( w \) and its derivative must vanish throughout this interval. Thus,
\[
v(x) u^{\prime}(x)-u(x) v^{\prime}(x)=0
\]
while (5) gives \( A(x)=B(x) \) for \( \alpha \leq x \leq \beta \).
For \( \alpha<x<\beta \), we may divide (6) by \( \nu(x)^{2} \) and conclude \( (u / v)^{\prime}=0 \), whence \( u / v \) is constant on \( (\alpha, \beta) \). Thus \( u \) and \( v \) are proportional on \( (\alpha, \beta) \). By continuity they are also proportional on \( [\alpha, \beta] \).

Remark. Using essentially the same argument, we reach the same conclusion if we drop the assumption that \( v(\alpha)=0 \) and instead assume
\[
u(\alpha)=v(\alpha), \quad u^{\prime}(\alpha)=v^{\prime}(\alpha) .
\]

This variation of the theorem is used in Problem P.M. 6 of the Twentysecond Competition.
For a more general treatment of the Sturm comparison theorem, see Coddington and Levinson, Theory of Ordinary Differential Equations, McGraw-Hill, New York, 1955, page 208 ff.

descriptive_long.question:
6. The curve \( graphfun=curvfunc(axisvar) \) passes through the origin with a slope of 1 . It satisfies the differential equation \( \left(axisvar^{2}+9\right) graphfun^{\prime \prime}+\left(axisvar^{2}+4\right) graphfun=0 \). Show that it crosses the \( axisvar \) axis between
\[
axisvar=\frac{3}{2} \pi \quad \text { and } \quad axisvar=\sqrt{\frac{63}{53}} \pi
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We shall use the Sturm comparison theorem. We state the theorem now and give the proof later.

Sturm Comparison Theorem. Let intervall be an interval in \( \mathbf{R} \) and suppose the functions \( compfunc \) and \( auxifun \) satisfy
\[
\begin{aligned}
compfunc^{\prime \prime}(axisvar)+bigcoef(axisvar) compfunc(axisvar) & =0 \\
auxifun^{\prime \prime}(axisvar)+smallcoef(axisvar) auxifun(axisvar) & =0
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( axisvar \in intervall \), where \( bigcoef \) and \( smallcoef \) are continuous functions such that \( bigcoef(axisvar) \geq smallcoef(axisvar) \) for \( axisvar \in intervall \). Assume \( auxifun \) is not identically zero on intervall and let \( leftzero \) and \( rightzero \) be zeros of \( auxifun \) with \( leftzero<rightzero \). Then there is a zero of \( compfunc \) in the open interval \( (leftzero, rightzero) \) unless \( bigcoef(axisvar)=smallcoef(axisvar) \) for \( leftzero \leq axisvar \leq rightzero \) and \( compfunc \) and \( auxifun \) are proportional in this interval.

For the stated problem we first compare the differential equations
\[
graphfun^{\prime \prime}+\frac{axisvar^{2}+4}{axisvar^{2}+9} graphfun=0
\]
and
\[
auxifun^{\prime \prime}+\frac{4}{9} auxifun=0 .
\]

As a solution of (2) we take \( sinefunc(axisvar)=\sin \frac{2}{3} axisvar \), which has zeros at 0 and \( \frac{5}{2} \pi \). Since
\[
\frac{axisvar^{2}+4}{axisvar^{2}+9} \geq \frac{4}{9}
\]
for all \( axisvar \) with strict inequality for \( axisvar \neq 0 \), any solution of (1) must have a zero in \( \left(0, \frac{3}{2} \pi\right) \). Hence there exists a \( crosspt \in\left(0, \frac{3}{2} \pi\right) \) such that \( curvfunc(crosspt)=0 \). Moreover, the graph of \( curvfunc \) must cross the \( axisvar \)-axis at \( crosspt \) because otherwise \( curvfunc^{\prime}(crosspt)=0 \), and then the uniqueness theorem for solutions of (1) would imply that \( curvfunc(axisvar)=0 \) for all \( axisvar \).
Now for \( 0 \leq axisvar \leq \frac{3}{2} \pi \), we have
\[
\frac{axisvar^{2}+4}{axisvar^{2}+9}<\frac{53}{63} .
\]

To see this, note that (3) is equivalent to \( 10 axisvar^{2}<225 \). Since \( \pi^{2}<10 \), we see that \( 10\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}\right)^{2}<225 \), so (3) follows.
If we set
\[
compfunc(axisvar)=\sin \sqrt{\frac{53}{63}} axisvar,
\]
then
\[
compfunc^{\prime \prime}(axisvar)+\frac{53}{63} compfunc(axisvar)=0 .
\]

Applying the Sturm comparison theorem again (with \( intervall=\left[0, \frac{3}{2} \pi\right] \) ), we conclude that \( compfunc \) has a zero on \( (0, crosspt) \). But the first positive zero of \( compfunc \) is at \( \sqrt{63 / 53} \pi \), so \( \sqrt{63 / 53} \pi<crosspt \). Thus we have
\[
\sqrt{\frac{63}{53}} \pi<crosspt<\frac{3}{2} \pi
\]
as required.
Proof of the Sturm Comparison Theorem. The zeros of \( auxifun \) are isolated, hence we may assume that \( rightzero \) is the next zero after \( leftzero \); i.e., that \( auxifun \) has a fixed sign on \( (leftzero, rightzero) \). Suppose \( compfunc \) has no zero in \( (leftzero, rightzero) \). Then \( compfunc \) also has a fixed sign on ( \( leftzero, rightzero \) ). Changing the signs of \( compfunc \) and/or \( auxifun \) if necessary (which does not affect the location of the zeros), we may assume that both \( compfunc \) and \( auxifun \) are positive on \( (leftzero, rightzero) \). It is then clear that \( auxifun^{\prime}(leftzero) \geq 0 \) and \( auxifun^{\prime}(rightzero) \leq 0 \). A non-zero solution of a non-singular second-order linear differential equation and its derivative cannot both vanish at the same point, so we must have
\[
auxifun^{\prime}(leftzero)>0, \quad auxifun^{\prime}(rightzero)<0 .
\]

Consider \( crossdet(axisvar)=compfunc(axisvar) auxifun^{\prime}(axisvar)-compfunc^{\prime}(axisvar) auxifun(axisvar) \). We have
\[
\begin{aligned}
crossdet^{\prime}(axisvar) & =compfunc(axisvar) auxifun^{\prime \prime}(axisvar)-compfunc^{\prime \prime}(axisvar) auxifun(axisvar) \\
& =(bigcoef(axisvar)-smallcoef(axisvar)) compfunc(axisvar) auxifun(axisvar) \geq 0
\end{aligned}
\]
for all \( axisvar \in[leftzero, rightzero] \). Therefore, \( crossdet \) is non-decreasing on \( [leftzero, rightzero] \), and in particular \( crossdet(leftzero) \leq crossdet(rightzero) \). Then from the definition of \( crossdet \) we obtain
\[
compfunc(leftzero) auxifun^{\prime}(leftzero) \leq compfunc(rightzero) auxifun^{\prime}(rightzero) .
\]

Comparing this with (4) and remembering that \( compfunc(leftzero) \geq 0, compfunc(rightzero) \geq 0 \), we see that \( compfunc(leftzero)=compfunc(rightzero)=0 \) and \( crossdet(leftzero)=crossdet(rightzero)=0 \). But then, since \( crossdet \) is monotone on \( [leftzero, rightzero] \), both \( crossdet \) and its derivative must vanish throughout this interval. Thus,
\[
auxifun(axisvar) compfunc^{\prime}(axisvar)-compfunc(axisvar) auxifun^{\prime}(axisvar)=0
\]
while (5) gives \( bigcoef(axisvar)=smallcoef(axisvar) \) for \( leftzero \leq axisvar \leq rightzero \).
For \( leftzero<axisvar<rightzero \), we may divide (6) by \( sinefunc(axisvar)^{2} \) and conclude \( (compfunc / auxifun)^{\prime}=0 \), whence \( compfunc / auxifun \) is constant on \( (leftzero, rightzero) \). Thus \( compfunc \) and \( auxifun \) are proportional on \( (leftzero, rightzero) \). By continuity they are also proportional on \( [leftzero, rightzero] \).

Remark. Using essentially the same argument, we reach the same conclusion if we drop the assumption that \( auxifun(leftzero)=0 \) and instead assume
\[
compfunc(leftzero)=auxifun(leftzero), \quad compfunc^{\prime}(leftzero)=auxifun^{\prime}(leftzero) .
\]

This variation of the theorem is used in Problem P.M. 6 of the Twentysecond Competition.
For a more general treatment of the Sturm comparison theorem, see Coddington and Levinson, Theory of Ordinary Differential Equations, McGraw-Hill, New York, 1955, page 208 ff.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
6. The curve \( roundabout=waterfall(turnpike) \) passes through the origin with a slope of 1 . It satisfies the differential equation \( \left(turnpike^{2}+9\right) roundabout^{\prime \prime}+\left(turnpike^{2}+4\right) roundabout=0 \). Show that it crosses the \( turnpike \) axis between
\[
turnpike=\frac{3}{2} \pi \quad \text { and } \quad turnpike=\sqrt{\frac{63}{53}} \pi
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. We shall use the Sturm comparison theorem. We state the theorem now and give the proof later.

Sturm Comparison Theorem. Let treescape be an interval in \( \mathbf{R} \) and suppose the functions \( lighthouse \) and \( windstorm \) satisfy
\[
\begin{aligned}
lighthouse^{\prime \prime}(turnpike)+partridge(turnpike) lighthouse(turnpike) & =0 \\
windstorm^{\prime \prime}(turnpike)+hucklebee(turnpike) windstorm(turnpike) & =0
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( turnpike \in treescape \), where \( partridge \) and \( hucklebee \) are continuous functions such that \( partridge(turnpike) \geq hucklebee(turnpike) \) for \( turnpike \in treescape \). Assume \( windstorm \) is not identically zero on treescape and let \( boulevard \) and \( carnation \) be zeros of \( windstorm \) with \( boulevard<carnation \). Then there is a zero of \( lighthouse \) in the open interval \( (boulevard, carnation) \) unless \( partridge(turnpike)=hucklebee(turnpike) \) for \( boulevard \leq turnpike \leq carnation \) and \( lighthouse \) and \( windstorm \) are proportional in this interval.

For the stated problem we first compare the differential equations
\[
roundabout^{\prime \prime}+\frac{turnpike^{2}+4}{turnpike^{2}+9} roundabout=0
\]
and
\[
windstorm^{\prime \prime}+\frac{4}{9} windstorm=0 .
\]

As a solution of (2) we take \( racehorse(turnpike)=\sin \frac{2}{3} turnpike \), which has zeros at 0 and \( \frac{5}{2} \pi \). Since
\[
\frac{turnpike^{2}+4}{turnpike^{2}+9} \geq \frac{4}{9}
\]
for all \( turnpike \) with strict inequality for \( turnpike \neq 0 \), any solution of (1) must have a zero in \( \left(0, \frac{3}{2} \pi\right) \). Hence there exists a \( crosswind \in\left(0, \frac{3}{2} \pi\right) \) such that \( waterfall(crosswind)=0 \). Moreover, the graph of \( waterfall \) must cross the \( turnpike \)-axis at \( crosswind \) because otherwise \( waterfall^{\prime}(crosswind)=0 \), and then the uniqueness theorem for solutions of (1) would imply that \( waterfall(turnpike)=0 \) for all \( turnpike \).
Now for \( 0 \leq turnpike \leq \frac{3}{2} \pi \), we have
\[
\frac{turnpike^{2}+4}{turnpike^{2}+9}<\frac{53}{63} .
\]

To see this, note that (3) is equivalent to \( 10 turnpike^{2}<225 \). Since \( \pi^{2}<10 \), we see that \( 10\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}\right)^{2}<225 \), so (3) follows.
If we set
\[
lighthouse(turnpike)=\sin \sqrt{\frac{53}{63}} turnpike,
\]
then
\[
lighthouse^{\prime \prime}(turnpike)+\frac{53}{63} lighthouse(turnpike)=0 .
\]

Applying the Sturm comparison theorem again (with \( treescape=\left[0, \frac{3}{2} \pi\right] \) ), we conclude that \( lighthouse \) has a zero on \( (0, crosswind) \). But the first positive zero of \( lighthouse \) is at \( \sqrt{63 / 53} \pi \), so \( \sqrt{63 / 53} \pi<crosswind \). Thus we have
\[
\sqrt{\frac{63}{53}} \pi<crosswind<\frac{3}{2} \pi
\]
as required.
Proof of the Sturm Comparison Theorem. The zeros of \( windstorm \) are isolated, hence we may assume that \( carnation \) is the next zero after \( boulevard \); i.e., that \( windstorm \) has a fixed sign on \( (boulevard, carnation) \). Suppose \( lighthouse \) has no zero in \( (boulevard, carnation) \). Then \( lighthouse \) also has a fixed sign on ( \( boulevard, carnation \) ). Changing the signs of \( lighthouse \) and/or \( windstorm \) if necessary (which does not affect the location of the zeros), we may assume that both \( lighthouse \) and \( windstorm \) are positive on \( (boulevard, carnation) \). It is then clear that \( windstorm^{\prime}(boulevard) \geq 0 \) and \( windstorm^{\prime}(carnation) \leq 0 \). A non-zero solution of a non-singular second-order linear differential equation and its derivative cannot both vanish at the same point, so we must have
\[
windstorm^{\prime}(boulevard)>0, \quad windstorm^{\prime}(carnation)<0 .
\]

Consider \( honeycomb(turnpike)=lighthouse(turnpike) windstorm^{\prime}(turnpike)-lighthouse^{\prime}(turnpike) windstorm(turnpike) \). We have
\[
\begin{aligned}
honeycomb^{\prime}(turnpike) & =lighthouse(turnpike) windstorm^{\prime \prime}(turnpike)-lighthouse^{\prime \prime}(turnpike) windstorm(turnpike) \\
& =(partridge(turnpike)-hucklebee(turnpike)) lighthouse(turnpike) windstorm(turnpike) \geq 0
\end{aligned}
\]
for all \( turnpike \in[ boulevard, carnation ] \). Therefore, \( honeycomb \) is non-decreasing on \( [boulevard, carnation] \), and in particular \( honeycomb(boulevard) \leq honeycomb(carnation) \). Then from the definition of \( honeycomb \) we obtain
\[
lighthouse(boulevard) windstorm^{\prime}(boulevard) \leq lighthouse(carnation) windstorm^{\prime}(carnation) .
\]

Comparing this with (4) and remembering that \( lighthouse(boulevard) \geq 0, lighthouse(carnation) \geq 0 \), we see that \( lighthouse(boulevard)=lighthouse(carnation)=0 \) and \( honeycomb(boulevard)=honeycomb(carnation)=0 \). But then, since \( honeycomb \) is monotone on \( [boulevard, carnation] \), both \( honeycomb \) and its derivative must vanish throughout this interval. Thus,
\[
windstorm(turnpike) lighthouse^{\prime}(turnpike)-lighthouse(turnpike) windstorm^{\prime}(turnpike)=0
\]
while (5) gives \( partridge(turnpike)=hucklebee(turnpike) \) for \( boulevard \leq turnpike \leq carnation \).
For \( boulevard<turnpike<carnation \), we may divide (6) by \( racehorse(turnpike)^{2} \) and conclude \( (lighthouse / windstorm)^{\prime}=0 \), whence \( lighthouse / windstorm \) is constant on \( (boulevard, carnation) \). Thus \( lighthouse \) and \( windstorm \) are proportional on \( (boulevard, carnation) \). By continuity they are also proportional on \( [boulevard, carnation] \).

Remark. Using essentially the same argument, we reach the same conclusion if we drop the assumption that \( windstorm(boulevard)=0 \) and instead assume
\[
lighthouse(boulevard)=windstorm(boulevard), \quad lighthouse^{\prime}(boulevard)=windstorm^{\prime}(boulevard) .
\]

This variation of the theorem is used in Problem P.M. 6 of the Twentysecond Competition.
For a more general treatment of the Sturm comparison theorem, see Coddington and Levinson, Theory of Ordinary Differential Equations, McGraw-Hill, New York, 1955, page 208 ff.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
6. The curve \( \originless=\turbulent(\dependent) \) passes through the origin with a slope of 1 . It satisfies the differential equation \( \left(\dependent^{2}+9\right) \originless^{\prime \prime}+\left(\dependent^{2}+4\right) \originless=0 \). Show that it crosses the \( \dependent \) axis between
\[
\dependent=\frac{3}{2} \pi \quad \text { and } \quad \dependent=\sqrt{\frac{63}{53}} \pi
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. We shall use the Sturm comparison theorem. We state the theorem now and give the proof later.

Sturm Comparison Theorem. Let unbounded be an interval in \( \mathbf{R} \) and suppose the functions \( disarray \) and \( staticity \) satisfy
\[
\begin{aligned}
disarray^{\prime \prime}(\dependent)+rebellion(\dependent)\,disarray(\dependent) & =0 \\
staticity^{\prime \prime}(\dependent)+compliance(\dependent)\,staticity(\dependent) & =0
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( \dependent \in unbounded \), where \( rebellion(\dependent) \geq compliance(\dependent) \) for \( \dependent \in unbounded \). Assume \( staticity \) is not identically zero on unbounded and let \( conclusion \) and \( initiality \) be zeros of \( staticity \) with \( conclusion<initiality \). Then there is a zero of \( disarray \) in the open interval \( (conclusion, initiality) \) unless \( rebellion(\dependent)=compliance(\dependent) \) for \( conclusion \leq \dependent \leq initiality \) and \( disarray \) and \( staticity \) are proportional in this interval.

For the stated problem we first compare the differential equations
\[
\originless^{\prime \prime}+\frac{\dependent^{2}+4}{\dependent^{2}+9}\,\originless=0
\]
and
\[
staticity^{\prime \prime}+\frac{4}{9}\,staticity=0 .
\]

As a solution of (2) we take \( stillness(\dependent)=\sin \frac{2}{3}\dependent \), which has zeros at 0 and \( \frac{5}{2} \pi \). Since
\[
\frac{\dependent^{2}+4}{\dependent^{2}+9} \geq \frac{4}{9}
\]
for all \( \dependent \) with strict inequality for \( \dependent \neq 0 \), any solution of (1) must have a zero in \( \left(0, \frac{3}{2} \pi\right) \). Hence there exists a \( certainty \in\left(0, \frac{3}{2} \pi\right) \) such that \( turbulent(certainty)=0 \). Moreover, the graph of \( turbulent \) must cross the \( \dependent \)-axis at \( certainty \) because otherwise \( turbulent^{\prime}(certainty)=0 \), and then the uniqueness theorem for solutions of (1) would imply that \( turbulent(\dependent)=0 \) for all \( \dependent \).
Now for \( 0 \leq \dependent \leq \frac{3}{2} \pi \), we have
\[
\frac{\dependent^{2}+4}{\dependent^{2}+9}<\frac{53}{63} .
\]

To see this, note that (3) is equivalent to \( 10 \dependent^{2}<225 \). Since \( \pi^{2}<10 \), we see that \( 10\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}\right)^{2}<225 \), so (3) follows.
If we set
\[
disarray(\dependent)=\sin \sqrt{\frac{53}{63}}\dependent,
\]
then
\[
disarray^{\prime \prime}(\dependent)+\frac{53}{63}\,disarray(\dependent)=0 .
\]

Applying the Sturm comparison theorem again (with \( unbounded=\left[0, \frac{3}{2} \pi\right] \) ), we conclude that \( disarray \) has a zero on \( (0, certainty) \). But the first positive zero of \( disarray \) is at \( \sqrt{63 / 53} \pi \), so \( \sqrt{63 / 53} \pi<certainty \). Thus we have
\[
\sqrt{\frac{63}{53}} \pi<certainty<\frac{3}{2} \pi
\]
as required.

Proof of the Sturm Comparison Theorem. The zeros of \( staticity \) are isolated, hence we may assume that \( initiality \) is the next zero after \( conclusion \); i.e., that \( staticity \) has a fixed sign on \( (conclusion, initiality) \). Suppose \( disarray \) has no zero in \( (conclusion, initiality) \). Then \( disarray \) also has a fixed sign on \( (conclusion, initiality) \). Changing the signs of \( disarray \) and/or \( staticity \) if necessary (which does not affect the location of the zeros), we may assume that both \( disarray \) and \( staticity \) are positive on \( (conclusion, initiality) \). It is then clear that \( staticity^{\prime}(conclusion) \geq 0 \) and \( staticity^{\prime}(initiality) \leq 0 \). A non-zero solution of a non-singular second-order linear differential equation and its derivative cannot both vanish at the same point, so we must have
\[
staticity^{\prime}(conclusion)>0, \quad staticity^{\prime}(initiality)<0 .
\]

Consider \( steadfast(\dependent)=disarray(\dependent)\,staticity^{\prime}(\dependent)-disarray^{\prime}(\dependent)\,staticity(\dependent) \). We have
\[
\begin{aligned}
steadfast^{\prime}(\dependent) & =disarray(\dependent)\,staticity^{\prime \prime}(\dependent)-disarray^{\prime \prime}(\dependent)\,staticity(\dependent) \\
& =(rebellion(\dependent)-compliance(\dependent))\,disarray(\dependent)\,staticity(\dependent) \geq 0
\end{aligned}
\]
for all \( \dependent \in[conclusion, initiality] \). Therefore, \( steadfast \) is non-decreasing on \( [conclusion, initiality] \), and in particular \( steadfast(conclusion) \leq steadfast(initiality) \). Then from the definition of \( steadfast \) we obtain
\[
disarray(conclusion)\,staticity^{\prime}(conclusion) \leq disarray(initiality)\,staticity^{\prime}(initiality) .
\]

Comparing this with (4) and remembering that \( disarray(conclusion) \geq 0, disarray(initiality) \geq 0 \), we see that \( disarray(conclusion)=disarray(initiality)=0 \) and \( steadfast(conclusion)=steadfast(initiality)=0 \). But then, since \( steadfast \) is monotone on \( [conclusion, initiality] \), both \( steadfast \) and its derivative must vanish throughout this interval. Thus,
\[
staticity(\dependent)\,disarray^{\prime}(\dependent)-disarray(\dependent)\,staticity^{\prime}(\dependent)=0
\]
while (5) gives \( rebellion(\dependent)=compliance(\dependent) \) for \( conclusion \leq \dependent \leq initiality \).
For \( conclusion<\dependent<initiality \), we may divide (6) by \( stillness(\dependent)^{2} \) and conclude \( (disarray / staticity)^{\prime}=0 \), whence \( disarray / staticity \) is constant on \( (conclusion, initiality) \). Thus \( disarray \) and \( staticity \) are proportional on \( (conclusion, initiality) \). By continuity they are also proportional on \( [conclusion, initiality] \).

Remark. Using essentially the same argument, we reach the same conclusion if we drop the assumption that \( staticity(conclusion)=0 \) and instead assume
\[
disarray(conclusion)=staticity(conclusion), \quad disarray^{\prime}(conclusion)=staticity^{\prime}(conclusion) .
\]

This variation of the theorem is used in Problem P.M. 6 of the Twentysecond Competition.
For a more general treatment of the Sturm comparison theorem, see Coddington and Levinson, Theory of Ordinary Differential Equations, McGraw-Hill, New York, 1955, page 208 ff.

garbled_string.question:
6. The curve \( hjgrksla=rialgspm(qzxwvtnp) \) passes through the origin with a slope of 1 . It satisfies the differential equation \( \left(qzxwvtnp^{2}+9\right) hjgrksla^{\prime \prime}+\left(qzxwvtnp^{2}+4\right) hjgrksla=0 \). Show that it crosses the \( qzxwvtnp \) axis between
\[
qzxwvtnp=\frac{3}{2} \pi \quad \text { and } \quad qzxwvtnp=\sqrt{\frac{63}{53}} \pi
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. We shall use the Sturm comparison theorem. We state the theorem now and give the proof later.

Sturm Comparison Theorem. Let blsodqye be an interval in \( \mathbf{R} \) and suppose the functions mcfvlqde and pzngktob satisfy
\[
\begin{aligned}
mcfvlqde^{\prime \prime}(qzxwvtnp)+zqirvdsp(qzxwvtnp)\, mcfvlqde(qzxwvtnp) & =0 \\
pzngktob^{\prime \prime}(qzxwvtnp)+akplmfxe(qzxwvtnp)\, pzngktob(qzxwvtnp) & =0
\end{aligned}
\]
for \( qzxwvtnp \in blsodqye \), where \( zqirvdsp \) and \( akplmfxe \) are continuous functions such that \( zqirvdsp(qzxwvtnp) \geq akplmfxe(qzxwvtnp) \) for \( qzxwvtnp \in blsodqye \). Assume pzngktob is not identically zero on blsodqye and let sebzyxoq and gnvawtud be zeros of pzngktob with \( sebzyxoq<gnvawtud \). Then there is a zero of mcfvlqde in the open interval \( (sebzyxoq, gnvawtud) \) unless \( zqirvdsp(qzxwvtnp)=akplmfxe(qzxwvtnp) \) for \( sebzyxoq \le qzxwvtnp \le gnvawtud \) and mcfvlqde and pzngktob are proportional in this interval.

For the stated problem we first compare the differential equations
\[
hjgrksla^{\prime \prime}+\frac{qzxwvtnp^{2}+4}{qzxwvtnp^{2}+9}\, hjgrksla=0
\]
and
\[
pzngktob^{\prime \prime}+\frac{4}{9}\, pzngktob=0 .
\]

As a solution of (2) we take \( ydrclsoq(qzxwvtnp)=\sin \frac{2}{3} qzxwvtnp \), which has zeros at 0 and \( \frac{5}{2} \pi \). Since
\[
\frac{qzxwvtnp^{2}+4}{qzxwvtnp^{2}+9} \geq \frac{4}{9}
\]
for all \( qzxwvtnp \) with strict inequality for \( qzxwvtnp \neq 0 \), any solution of (1) must have a zero in \( \left(0, \frac{3}{2} \pi\right) \). Hence there exists a \( kjupehns \in\left(0, \frac{3}{2} \pi\right) \) such that \( rialgspm(kjupehns)=0 \). Moreover, the graph of rialgspm must cross the \( qzxwvtnp \)-axis at kjupehns because otherwise \( rialgspm^{\prime}(kjupehns)=0 \), and then the uniqueness theorem for solutions of (1) would imply that \( rialgspm(qzxwvtnp)=0 \) for all \( qzxwvtnp \).
Now for \( 0 \leq qzxwvtnp \leq \frac{3}{2} \pi \), we have
\[
\frac{qzxwvtnp^{2}+4}{qzxwvtnp^{2}+9}<\frac{53}{63} .
\]

To see this, note that (3) is equivalent to \( 10 qzxwvtnp^{2}<225 \). Since \( \pi^{2}<10 \), we see that \( 10\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}\right)^{2}<225 \), so (3) follows.
If we set
\[
mcfvlqde(qzxwvtnp)=\sin \sqrt{\frac{53}{63}}\, qzxwvtnp,
\]
then
\[
mcfvlqde^{\prime \prime}(qzxwvtnp)+\frac{53}{63}\, mcfvlqde(qzxwvtnp)=0 .
\]

Applying the Sturm comparison theorem again (with \( blsodqye=\left[0, \frac{3}{2} \pi\right] \) ), we conclude that mcfvlqde has a zero on \( (0, kjupehns) \). But the first positive zero of mcfvlqde is at \( \sqrt{63 / 53}\, \pi \), so \( \sqrt{63 / 53}\, \pi<kjupehns \). Thus we have
\[
\sqrt{\frac{63}{53}} \pi<kjupehns<\frac{3}{2} \pi
\]
as required.

Proof of the Sturm Comparison Theorem. The zeros of pzngktob are isolated, hence we may assume that gnvawtud is the next zero after sebzyxoq; i.e., that pzngktob has a fixed sign on \( (sebzyxoq, gnvawtud) \). Suppose mcfvlqde has no zero in \( (sebzyxoq, gnvawtud) \). Then mcfvlqde also has a fixed sign on \( (sebzyxoq, gnvawtud) \). Changing the signs of mcfvlqde and/or pzngktob if necessary (which does not affect the location of the zeros), we may assume that both mcfvlqde and pzngktob are positive on \( (sebzyxoq, gnvawtud) \). It is then clear that \( pzngktob^{\prime}(sebzyxoq) \geq 0 \) and \( pzngktob^{\prime}(gnvawtud) \leq 0 \). A non-zero solution of a non-singular second-order linear differential equation and its derivative cannot both vanish at the same point, so we must have
\[
pzngktob^{\prime}(sebzyxoq)>0, \quad pzngktob^{\prime}(gnvawtud)<0 .
\]

Consider \( nsvtqhma(qzxwvtnp)=mcfvlqde(qzxwvtnp)\, pzngktob^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp)-mcfvlqde^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp)\, pzngktob(qzxwvtnp) \). We have
\[
\begin{aligned}
nsvtqhma^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp) & =mcfvlqde(qzxwvtnp)\, pzngktob^{\prime \prime}(qzxwvtnp)-mcfvlqde^{\prime \prime}(qzxwvtnp)\, pzngktob(qzxwvtnp) \\
& =(zqirvdsp(qzxwvtnp)-akplmfxe(qzxwvtnp))\, mcfvlqde(qzxwvtnp)\, pzngktob(qzxwvtnp) \geq 0
\end{aligned}
\]
for all \( qzxwvtnp \in[sebzyxoq, gnvawtud] \). Therefore, nsvtqhma is non-decreasing on \( [sebzyxoq, gnvawtud] \), and in particular \( nsvtqhma(sebzyxoq) \leq nsvtqhma(gnvawtud) \). Then from the definition of nsvtqhma we obtain
\[
mcfvlqde(sebzyxoq)\, pzngktob^{\prime}(sebzyxoq) \leq mcfvlqde(gnvawtud)\, pzngktob^{\prime}(gnvawtud) .
\]

Comparing this with (4) and remembering that \( mcfvlqde(sebzyxoq) \geq 0, mcfvlqde(gnvawtud) \geq 0 \), we see that \( mcfvlqde(sebzyxoq)=mcfvlqde(gnvawtud)=0 \) and \( nsvtqhma(sebzyxoq)=nsvtqhma(gnvawtud)=0 \). But then, since nsvtqhma is monotone on \( [sebzyxoq, gnvawtud] \), both nsvtqhma and its derivative must vanish throughout this interval. Thus,
\[
pzngktob(qzxwvtnp)\, mcfvlqde^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp)-mcfvlqde(qzxwvtnp)\, pzngktob^{\prime}(qzxwvtnp)=0
\]
while (5) gives \( zqirvdsp(qzxwvtnp)=akplmfxe(qzxwvtnp) \) for \( sebzyxoq \leq qzxwvtnp \leq gnvawtud \).
For \( sebzyxoq<qzxwvtnp<gnvawtud \), we may divide (6) by \( ydrclsoq(qzxwvtnp)^{2} \) and conclude \( (mcfvlqde / pzngktob)^{\prime}=0 \), whence \( mcfvlqde / pzngktob \) is constant on \( (sebzyxoq, gnvawtud) \). Thus mcfvlqde and pzngktob are proportional on \( (sebzyxoq, gnvawtud) \). By continuity they are also proportional on \( [sebzyxoq, gnvawtud] \).

Remark. Using essentially the same argument, we reach the same conclusion if we drop the assumption that \( pzngktob(sebzyxoq)=0 \) and instead assume
\[
mcfvlqde(sebzyxoq)=pzngktob(sebzyxoq), \quad mcfvlqde^{\prime}(sebzyxoq)=pzngktob^{\prime}(sebzyxoq) .
\]

This variation of the theorem is used in Problem P.M. 6 of the Twentysecond Competition.
For a more general treatment of the Sturm comparison theorem, see Coddington and Levinson, Theory of Ordinary Differential Equations, McGraw-Hill, New York, 1955, page 208 ff.

kernel_variant.question:
Let the twice-differentiable curve \(y=f(x)\) satisfy
\[
      (x^{2}+25)\,y''(x)+
      (x^{2}+ 9)\,y(x)=0,\qquad f(0)=0,\;f'(0)=2\; .
\]
Show that its first positive zero \(\xi\) lies strictly between
\[
      x=\pi\sqrt{\tfrac{10}{7}}\quad\text{and}\quad x=\tfrac{5\pi}{3}.
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Rewrite the given equation as
\[
     y'' + q(x)\,y = 0,\qquad q(x) = \frac{x^2 + 9}{x^2 + 25}.	ag{1}
\]

Step 1. A constant lower bound.
Observe
\[
 q(x) = 1 - \frac{16}{x^2 + 25} \ge \frac{9}{25},
\]
with equality only at x = 0.  Set
\[
 m := \frac{9}{25},\quad \sqrt m = \frac{3}{5}.
\]

Step 2. Upper bound for \xi .
Consider v'' + m v = 0, whose solution v(x) = sin(3x/5) has its first two zeros at x = 0 and x = 5\pi /3.  Since q(x) \geq  m on [0,5\pi /3] with strict inequality on (0,5\pi /3), Sturm's comparison theorem guarantees that any nontrivial solution of (1)---in particular f---has a zero in (0,5\pi /3).  Hence
\[
   \xi  < \frac{5\pi }{3}.
\]

Step 3. A constant upper bound for q on [0,5\pi /3].
We check
\[
 10(x^2 + 9) < 7(x^2 + 25)
 \iff 3x^2 < 85,
\]
and indeed (5\pi /3)^2 \approx  27.4 < 85/3 \approx  28.3.  Thus
\[
 q(x) < M := \frac{7}{10},\quad \sqrt M = \sqrt{\tfrac{7}{10}},\quad
 \text{for }0 \le x \le 5\pi /3.
\]

Step 4. Lower bound for \xi .
Solve u'' + M u = 0 \Rightarrow  u(x) = sin(\sqrt{M} x).  Its first positive zero is
\[
 a = \frac{\pi }{\sqrt{M}} = \pi \sqrt{\tfrac{10}{7}}.
\]
Suppose to the contrary that \xi  \leq  a.  Then f has zeros at 0 and \xi  \leq  a, and on [0,\xi ] we have q(x) < M.  Sturm's theorem (in contrapositive form) would force u to have a zero in (0,\xi ), contradicting that its first zero is at x = a.  Therefore \xi  > a.

Step 5. Bracketing \xi .
Combining the two inequalities gives the desired result:
\[
   \pi \sqrt{\tfrac{10}{7}} < \xi  < \frac{5\pi }{3}.
\]
Thus the first positive zero of f lies strictly between \pi \sqrt{10/7} and 5\pi /3, as required.

core_steps: Rewrite ODE as y'' + q(x) y = 0 with q(x) = (xÂ²+Î²)/(xÂ²+Î±).
Choose constant m â‰¤ q(x) for all x; compare with solution sin(âˆšm x) via Sturm to force a zero before its next sine-zero.
Show q(x) < M on [0, X]; compare with sin(âˆšM x) to locate zero after the first sine-zero.
Combine the two inequalities to sandwich the first positive zero Î¾ of y between the two sine-zeros.

slot1.description: Positive constants added to xÂ² in the numerator and denominator of q(x).  Requirement: Î±>Î²>0.

slot2.description: Global lower bound m = Î²/Î± used in the first Sturm comparison ODE v'' + m v = 0.

slot3.description: Right-end X of the interval on which the upper bound comparison is carried out.

slot4.description: Upper bound M that satisfies q(x) < M on [0, X] and is used in the second Sturm comparison.

slot5.description: First positive zero of sin(âˆšM x), giving the lower endpoint of the bracket for Î¾.

slot6.description: Initial slope of f at the origin; any non-zero value merely fixes the initial sign.
